blocks.label: VIII
blocks.html: VIII The finiteness theorem

pages.title: A biduality spectral sequence
pages.html:
1 A bidualité spectral sequence

Let us state the result we wish to reach:

Proposition 1.1

Let \(A\) be a noetherian ring and let \(I\) be an ideal of \(A\). Set \(X=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(Y=V(I)\). Let \(M\) be an \(A\)-module of finite type and of finite projective dimension. Let \({\operatorname{F}}=\widetilde{M}\) be the \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module associated to \(M\).

  1. 1)

    There exists a spectral sequence:

    \[ \operatorname{H}_Y(X, {\operatorname{F}}) \Longleftarrow \operatorname{Ext}^p_Y(\operatorname{Ext}^{-q}(M, A), A). \]
  2. 2)

    There exists a spectral sequence:

    \[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y(X, {\operatorname{F}}) \Longleftarrow \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^p_Y(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{-q}({\operatorname{F}}, \mathscr{O}_X), \mathscr{O}_X). \]

Of course, 2) follows from 1) by noting that, if \(M\) and \(N\) are two \(A\)-modules of finite type and if one sets \({{\operatorname{F}}}=\widetilde{M}\) and \({{\operatorname{G}}}=\widetilde{N}\), one has isomorphisms:

\begin{align*} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits_Y({\operatorname{F}}) &\approx \widetilde{\operatorname{H}_Y(X, {\operatorname{F}})}, \\ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_Y({\operatorname{F}}, {\operatorname{G}}) &\approx \widetilde{\operatorname{Ext}_Y({\operatorname{F}}, {\operatorname{G}}}), \\ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits_{\mathscr{O}_X}({\operatorname{F}}, {\operatorname{G}}) &\approx \widetilde{\operatorname{Ext}_A(M, N)}. \end{align*}

Let \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}\) be the category of \(A\)-modules, and \(\underline{\mathrm{Ab}}\) that of abelian groups. Let \({\underline{\mathrm{F}}}\) be the functor:

\begin{align*} \underline{\mathrm{F}}: \underline{\mathrm{C}}&\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{Ab}} \quad\text{defined by} \\ M &\rightsquigarrow \Gamma_Y(\widetilde{M}). \end{align*}

We know since Exposé II that there exists an isomorphism of \(\partial\)-functors:

\[ \operatorname{H}^\ast_Y(X, \widetilde{M})\approx \operatorname{R}^\ast {\underline{\mathrm{F}}}(M). \]

Moreover, let \(\operatorname{Ext}^\ast_Y\) be the right derived functors in the second argument of

\[ {\underline{\mathrm{F}}}\circ \operatorname{Hom}\colon \underline{\mathrm{C}}^\circ \times \underline{\mathrm{C}} \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \underline{\mathrm{Ab}}. \]

We know since Exposé VI that one has an isomorphism of \(\partial\)-functors:

\[ \operatorname{Ext}^\ast_Y(M, N)\simeq \operatorname{Ext}^\ast_Y(\widetilde{M}, \widetilde{N}). \]

Let us finally recall the following result of Exposé VI: if \(C\) is an injective \(A\)-module and if \(N\) is an \(A\)-module of finite type, the sheaf \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}}\nolimits(\widetilde{N}, \widetilde{C})\approx\widetilde{\operatorname{Hom}(N, C)}\) is flasque, hence \(\operatorname{R}^1{\underline{\mathrm{F}}}(\operatorname{Hom}(N, C))=0\).

It remains for us to prove the following result:

Lemma 1.2

Let \(A\) be a noetherian ring and let \(\underline{\mathrm{C}}\) be the category of \(A\)-modules. Let \({\underline{\mathrm{F}}}\colon \underline{\mathrm{C}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}\underline{\mathrm{Ab}}\) be a left exact additive functor such that, for every \(A\)-module \(N\) and every injective \(A\)-module \(C\), one has \(\operatorname{R}^1{\underline{\mathrm{F}}}(\operatorname{Hom}(N, C))=0\). Let \(M\) be an \(A\)-module of finite type and of finite projective dimension. There exists a spectral sequence:

\[ \operatorname{R}^\ast {\underline{\mathrm{F}}}(M)\Longleftarrow \operatorname{Ext}^p_{{\underline{\mathrm{F}}}}(\operatorname{Ext}^{-q}(M, A), A), \]

where \(\operatorname{Ext}^p_{{\underline{\mathrm{F}}}}\) denotes the \(p\)-th right derived functor of \({\underline{\mathrm{F}}}\circ \operatorname{Hom}\).

We shall consider only complexes whose differential is of degree \(+1\). By the hypothesis made on \(M\), there exists a free resolution of \(M\) of finite length:

\[ u\colon L^\bullet \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M, \]

where, moreover, the \(L^p\) are modules of finite type and \(L^p=0\) if \(p\not\in[-n, 0]\). Let furthermore \(v\colon M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} I^\bullet\) be an injective resolution of \(M\). I claim that

\[ \tag{1.1} v\circ u\colon L^\bullet\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} I^\bullet \]

is an injective resolution of \(L^\bullet\). It is appropriate to make precise what this means.

Definition 1.3

Let \(X^\bullet\) be a complex of \(A\)-modules; by an injective resolution of \(X^\bullet\) one means a homomorphism of complexes:

\[ x\colon X^\bullet\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} CX^\bullet, \]

such that \(CX^p\) is injective for every \(p\in \mathbf{Z}\), and such that \(x\) induces an isomorphism in homology.

Proposition 1.4

Every complex bounded on the left, i.e. such that there exists \(q\in\mathbf{Z}\) with \(X^p=0\) for \(p<q\), admits an injective resolution. Moreover, if \(u\colon X^\bullet\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y^\bullet\) is a homomorphism of complexes (bounded on the left) and if \(x\colon X^\bullet \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} CX^\bullet\) and \(y\colon Y^\bullet\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} CY^\bullet\) are injective resolutions of \(X^\bullet\) and \(Y^\bullet\), there exists a homomorphism of complexes:

\[ Cu\colon CX^\bullet\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} CY^\bullet, \]

unique up to homotopy, such that the diagram:

\[ \xymatrix{ X^\bullet \ar[r]^x\ar[d]_u & CX^\bullet\ar[d]^{Cu} \\ Y^\bullet \ar[r]^y & CY^\bullet \\ } \]

is commutative up to homotopy.

The proof is left to the reader1.

Let us recall a notation introduced in Exposé V.

Notation

Let \(X^\bullet\) and \(Y^\bullet\) be two complexes. One denotes by \(\operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(X^\bullet, Y^\bullet)\) the simple complex whose component of degree \(n\) is

\[ (\operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(X^\bullet, Y^\bullet))^n=\prod_{-p+q=n}\operatorname{Hom}(X^p, Y^q) \]

also denoted \(\operatorname{Hom}^n(X^\bullet, Y^\bullet)\), and whose differential is given by:

\begin{gather*} \partial_n\colon \operatorname{Hom}^n(X^\bullet, Y^\bullet)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}^{n+1}(X^\bullet, Y^\bullet)\\ \partial_n=d'+(-1)^{n+1}d'', \end{gather*}

where \(d'\) and \(d''\) are the differentials (of degree \(+1\)) induced by those of \(X^\bullet\) and of \(Y^\bullet\).

Let then \(A^\bullet\) be the complex defined by \(A^p=0\) if \(p\neq 0\) and \(A^0=A\). Let

\[ a\colon A^\bullet \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} CA^\bullet \]

be an injective resolution of \(A^\bullet\). Consider the double complex:

\[ \tag{1.2} Q^{p, q}=\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{Hom}(L^q, A), CA^p). \]

The first spectral sequence of the bicomplex \({\underline{\mathrm{F}}}Q^{\bullet\bullet}\) will give the conclusion of Lemma 1.2.

Set

\[ \tag{1.3} {L'}^\bullet=\operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(L^\bullet, A^\bullet), \]

and

\[ \tag{1.4} P^\bullet=\operatorname{Hom}^\bullet({L'}^\bullet, CA^\bullet). \]

One sees easily that \(P^\bullet\) is the simple complex associated to \(Q^{\bullet\bullet}\). Let us compute the abutment of the spectral sequence, i.e. the homology of \({\underline{\mathrm{F}}}P^\bullet\). For this, using the fact that \(L^\bullet\) is free of finite type in every dimension, one proves that \(L^\bullet\) is isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Hom}^\bullet({L'}^\bullet, A^\bullet)\). From the homomorphism \(a\colon A^\bullet\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} CA^\bullet\), one deduces a homomorphism:

\[ b\colon \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet({L'}^\bullet, A^\bullet)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}^\bullet({L'}^\bullet, CA^\bullet), \]

or again, a homomorphism

\[ \tag{1.5} c\colon L^\bullet\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} P^\bullet. \]

This said, it is easy to see, using the fact that \({L'}^\bullet\) is free of finite type in every dimension and bounded on the left, that (1.5) is an injective resolution of \(L^\bullet\). Using Proposition 1.4., one concludes that \(P^\bullet\) is homotopically equivalent to \(I^\bullet\), where \(I^\bullet\) is the injective resolution of \(M\) introduced above (1.1). One deduces that the abutment of the first spectral sequence of the double complex \({\underline{\mathrm{F}}}Q^{\bullet\bullet}\), which is \(\operatorname{H}^\ast({\underline{\mathrm{F}}}P^\bullet)\), is isomorphic to \(\operatorname{R}^\ast {\underline{\mathrm{F}}}(M)\).

The initial term of the first spectral sequence of the bicomplex \({\underline{\mathrm{F}}}Q^{\bullet\bullet}\) is:

\[ \operatorname{E}^{p, q}_2 = {'\!\operatorname{H}}^p({''\!\operatorname{H}}^q({\underline{\mathrm{F}}}Q^{\bullet\bullet})). \]

For every \(p\in\mathbf{Z}\), \(CA^p\) is injective. By the hypothesis made on \({\underline{\mathrm{F}}}\), the functor:

\[ N \rightsquigarrow {\underline{\mathrm{F}}}\operatorname{Hom}(N, CA^p) \]

is exact. Whence one deduces isomorphisms:

\[ {''\!\operatorname{H}}^q({\underline{\mathrm{F}}}\operatorname{Hom}({L'}^\bullet, CA^p))\approx {\underline{\mathrm{F}}}\operatorname{Hom}(\operatorname{H}^{-q}({L'}^\bullet), CA^p). \]

By the definition of \(\operatorname{Ext}^\ast_{{\underline{\mathrm{F}}}}\) as the derived functor of \({\underline{\mathrm{F}}}{^\ast}\operatorname{Hom}\), one deduces isomorphisms:

\[ \operatorname{E}^{p, q}_2 \approx \operatorname{Ext}^p_{{\underline{\mathrm{F}}}}(\operatorname{H}^q({L'}^\bullet), A). \]

Now \({L'}^\bullet=\operatorname{Hom}^\bullet(L^\bullet, A^\bullet)\), where \(L^\bullet\) is a free resolution of \(M\), whence isomorphisms:

\[ \operatorname{Ext}^{q}(M, A)\approx \operatorname{H}^q({L'}^\bullet), \]

which gives the conclusion. Q.E.D.

pages.title: The finiteness theorem
pages.html:
2 The finiteness theorem

Theorem 2.1

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed part of \(X\) and \(F\) a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Suppose that \(X\) is locally immersible in a regular prescheme1. Let \(i\in\mathbf{Z}\). Suppose that:

  1. for every \(x\in U=X-Y\), we have

    \[ \operatorname{H}^{i-c(x)}({F}_x)=0, \]

    where we have set:

    \[ \tag{2.1} c(x)=\operatorname{codim} (\overline{\{x\}}\cap Y, \overline{\{x\}}). \]

    Then:

  2. \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i_Y(F)\) is coherent.

Corollary 2.2

Under the hypotheses of the preceding theorem, condition a) is equivalent to:

  1. c)

    for every \(x\in U\) such that \(c(x)=1\), we have \(\operatorname{H}^{i-1}(F_x)=0\).

Corollary 2.3

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, locally immersible in a regular prescheme, \(Y\) a closed part of \(X\), \(F\) a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module, \(n\) an integer. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. for every \(x\in U\), we have \(\operatorname{prof} F_x>n-c(x)\);

  2. for every \(x\in U\) such that \(c(x)=1\), we have \(\operatorname{prof} F_x\geqslant n\);

  3. for every \(i\in \mathbf{Z}\), \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i_Y(F)\) is coherent if \(i\leqslant n\).

  4. \(\operatorname{R}^ii_\ast(F_{|U})\) is coherent for \(i<n\) .

Suppose these results established when \(X\) is the spectrum of a regular noetherian ring \(A\) and when \(F\) is the sheaf associated to an \(A\)-module of finite projective dimension.

Let us first remark that, if \((X_j)_{j\in J}\) is an open covering of \(X\) by open sets, each of the conditions above is equivalent to the conjunction of the analogous conditions obtained by replacing \(X\) by \(X_j\), \(Y\) by \(Y_j=Y\cap X_j\) and \(F\) by \(F{|X_j}\). Indeed, only the conditions involving \(c(x)\) can present a difficulty. Let \(x\in U\). If \(x\in X_j\), set

\[ c_j(x)=\operatorname{codim}(X_j\cap \overline{\{x\}}\cap Y, X_j\cap\overline{\{x\}}), \]

we necessarily have \(c_j(x)\geqslant c(x)\). Let \(y\in \overline{\{x\}}\cap Y\) "give the codimension", i.e. such that \(c(x)=\dim \mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y}\), let \(X_j\) be an open set of the covering such that \(y\in X_j\), then \(x\in X_j\), hence \(c_j(x)=c(x)\), which allows us to conclude.

We choose a covering of \(X\) by open sets immersible in a regular prescheme. Applying what precedes, we see that we may suppose \(X\) closed in a regular \(X'\). The reduction to \(X'\) is then immediate.

We may therefore suppose \(X\) regular, and even affine by covering \(X\) by affine open sets. That we may suppose \(F=\widetilde{M}\), where \(M\) is of finite projective dimension, will result from the following lemma:

Lemma 2.4

Let \(X\) be a regular noetherian prescheme. Let \(F\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. The function which to every \(x\in X\) associates the projective dimension of \(F_x\) is bounded above.

Indeed, let \(x\in X\) and let \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(x\). Let \(L^\bullet\) be a projective resolution of the module \(F(U)\), where the \(L^i\) are of finite type. By hypothesis, the ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) is regular, hence the projective dimension of \(F_x\) is finite; let \(d\) be this integer. Let

\[ K=\ker(L^{d-1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L^{d-2}). \]

The module \(K_x\) is free, since \(d\) is the projective dimension of \(F_x\) ([M], Ch. VI. Prop. 2.1). By (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{I}}\) 5.4.1 Errata), we deduce that the \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Module \(\widetilde{K}\) is free on a neighbourhood \(U'\) of \(x\), \(U'\subset U\). Choosing \(f\in \mathscr{O}_X(U)\) such that \(D(f)\subset U'\), we thus have a projective resolution of \(M_{f'}\) (\(M=F(U)\)):

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K_f \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (L^{d-1})_f\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M_f\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

which proves that the function under study is upper semi-continuous. Now \(X\) is quasi-compact, whence the conclusion.

We now suppose \(X\) affine noetherian regular and we suppose that \(F=\widetilde{M}\), where \(M\) is an \(A\)-module of finite type, necessarily of finite projective dimension. We shall proceed in several steps. First, we find a condition d), equivalent to a), and prove that it is also equivalent to c). Then, with the help of the spectral sequence of the preceding n°, we prove d) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) b). It then remains to prove that (iii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii); indeed, (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (iii) follows immediately from a) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) c) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) b).

Let \(x\in U\); by hypothesis \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) is a regular local ring; denoting by \(D\) the dualizing functor relative to the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\), it follows from (V 2.1) that:

\[ D\operatorname{H}^{i-c(x)}(F_x)\approx \operatorname{Ext}^{d(x)-i}_{\mathscr{O}_{X, x}}(F_x, \mathscr{O}_{X, x}), \]

where we have set

\[ \tag{2.2} d(x)=\dim \mathscr{O}_{X, x} + c(x) = \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, x}+\operatorname{codim}(\overline{\{x\}}\cap Y, \overline{\{x\}}). \]

Now \(X\) is noetherian and \(F\) coherent, hence:

\[ \tag{2.3} D\operatorname{H}^{i-c(x)}(F_x)\approx (\operatorname{Ext}^{d(x)-i}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, \mathscr{O}_X))_x. \]

Moreover, for a module to be zero, it is necessary and sufficient that its dual be so. For every \(q\in\mathbf{Z}\), set:

\[ \tag{2.4} \begin{cases} S_q=\operatorname{Supp} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^q_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, \mathscr{O}_X), \\ S'_q=S_q\cap U\text{, (}U=X-Y\text{)}, \\ Z_q=\overline{S'_q}\cap Y. \end{cases} \]

From formula (2.3), it follows that a) and c) are respectively equivalent to

  1. a\('\))

    for every \(q\in\mathbf{Z}\) and every \(x\in S'_q\), we have \(q+i\neq d(x)\).

  2. c\('\))

    for every \(q\in\mathbf{Z}\) and every \(x\in S'_q\), if \(c(x)=1\), we have \(q+i\neq d(x)\).

Here is the condition d) promised above:

  1. d)

    for every \(q\in \mathbf{Z}\) and every \(y\in Z_q\), we have \(q+i\neq \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, y}\).

These conditions are equivalent:

a\('\)) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) c\('\)) for the record.

d) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) a\('\)). Indeed, let \(q\in \mathbf{Z}\) and let \(x\in S'_q\); let \(y\in\overline{\{x\}}\cap Y\) which "gives the codimension", i.e. such that:

\[ \tag{2.5} \dim \mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y}=\operatorname{codim}(\overline{\{x\}}\cap Y, \overline{\{x\}})=c(x). \]

From the fact that \(X\) is regular at \(y\), we deduce:

\[ \tag{2.6} \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, y}=d(x)\quad \text{(cf. (2.2)).} \]

But \(y\in\overline{\{x\}}\), hence \(y\in Z_q\), whence the conclusion.

c\('\)) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) d). Let \(q\in\mathbf{Z}\) and let \(y\in Z_q\). Let us provisionally admit that there exists \(x\in S'_q\) such that:

\[ \dim \mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y}=1\, \text{;} \]

(we also say that \(x\) follows \(y\)). It follows that \(c(x)=1\), since \(y\) "gives the codimension of \(\overline{\{x\}}\cap Y\) in \(\overline{\{x\}}\)", since \(x\not\in Y\). By c\('\)) we derive

\[ q+i\neq d(x). \]

Whence the conclusion, if we remark that \(d(x)=\dim \mathscr{O}_{X, y}\) (2.6). The admitted result is expressed in the following lemma:

Lemma 2.5

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and let \(Y\) be a closed part of \(X\). Set \(U=X-Y\) and suppose that \(U\) is dense in \(X\). For every \(y\in Y\), there exists \(x\in U\) "which follows it", i.e. such that:

\[ y\in\overline{\{x\}}\text{ and } \dim\mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y}=1. \]

We have applied the lemma by taking for \(X\) the prescheme \(\overline{S'_q}\) and for \(Y\) the part \(Y\cap \overline{S'_q}\).

Proof of 2.5▾

There exists \(x\in U\) such that \(y\in\overline{\{x\}}\); let us therefore choose an \(x\in U\) such that \(y\in\overline{\{x\}}\) and such that \(\dim \mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y}=r\) is minimal. It must be proved that \(r=1\). Since \(x\) has been chosen so that every \(z\in\overline{\{x\}}\), \(z\neq x\), is in \(Y\), \(\{x\}\) is open in \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y})\). Whence the conclusion.

Q.E.D.

The second step consists in deducing b) from d).

Set \(D(Z_q)=\{\dim \mathscr{O}_{X, y} \mid y\in Z_q\}\). By d), we know that, for every \(q\in\mathbf{Z}\), we have \(q+i\not\in D(Z_q)\). We then apply 2.2, and we see that

\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{q+i}_Y(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^q(F, \mathscr{O}_X), \mathscr{O}_X) \text{ is coherent.} \]

The initial term of the spectral sequence of the preceding n° is given by:

\[ \operatorname{E}^{p, q}_2=\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^p_Y(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{-q}(F, \mathscr{O}_X), \mathscr{O}_X). \]

We deduce that \(\operatorname{E}^{p, q}_2\) is coherent for every \(p\in\mathbf{Z}\) and every \(q\in\mathbf{Z}\) such that \(p+q=i\). Now there are only a finite number of pairs \((p, q)\) such that \(p+q=i\), and this spectral sequence converges to \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^\ast_Y(F)\), whence the conclusion.

It remains for us to prove that (iii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). Let us denote:

\[ i\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \]

the canonical immersion of \(U\) into \(X\). Taking into account the exact homology sequence of the closed set \(Y\) (I 2.11) we see that (iii) is equivalent to:

(iv) \(\operatorname{R}^ii_\ast(F_{|U})\) is coherent for \(i<n\).

Indeed, we have an exact sequence:

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^0_Y(F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_\ast(F_{|U})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^1_Y(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0. \]

Now \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^0_Y(F)\) is a quasi-coherent subsheaf of \(F\) which is coherent, hence is coherent. Therefore \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^1_Y(F)\) is coherent if and only if \(i_\ast(F_{|U})\) is. Moreover, if \(p>0\), the exact cohomology sequence of the closed set \(Y\) reduces to isomorphisms:

\[ \operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(F_{|U})\overset{\approx}{\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}}\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^{p+1}_Y(F). \]

We shall prove that (iv) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). For this, let us recall (ii):

(ii) for every \(x\in U\) such that \(c(x)=1\), we have \(\operatorname{prof} F_x\geqslant n\).

Let us reason by induction on \(n\).

If \(n=0\), the two conditions are empty.

If \(n=1\), we suppose that \(i_\ast(F{|U})\) is coherent. Let us reason by contradiction and suppose that there exists \(x\in U\) such that \(c(x)=1\) and \(\operatorname{prof} F_x=0\), i.e. \(x\in \operatorname{Ass} F_x\). Let \(y\in\overline{\{x\}}\cap Y\) such that \(\dim \mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y}=1\). Set:

\[ A=\mathscr{O}_{X, y}\text{ and } X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A). \]

Let us perform the base change \(v\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), which is flat.

\[ \tag{2.7} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix@C=5em{ U'=X'\times_X U\ar[r]^-{v'}\ar[d]_{i'} & U\ar[d]^i \\ X'\ar[r]^-v & \,X\,. } \end{array} \]

The morphism \(i\) is separated (since it is an immersion), and of finite type (since it is an open immersion and \(X\) is locally noetherian), the base change is flat hence (EGA III 1.4.15) we have an isomorphism:

\[ \tag{2.8} v^\ast(i_\ast(F|U) \approx i'_\ast({v'}^\ast(F_{|U})). \]

Let us denote by \(\underline x\) (resp. \(\underline y\)) the ideal of \(A\) corresponding to \(x\) (resp. \(y\)).

Set \({\operatorname{G}}={v'}^\ast(F{}_{|U})\); \({\operatorname{G}}\) is coherent and \(\underline{x}\in\operatorname{Ass} {\operatorname{G}}\), hence there exists a monomorphism \(\mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {\operatorname{G}}\), and consequently \(i'_\ast(\mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}}{}_{|U'})\) is coherent. By the choice of \(y\), \(\dim A/\underline x=1\), and consequently the support of \(\mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}}\) is reduced to \(\overline{\{\underline x\}}= \{\underline x\}\cup \{\underline y\}\), since \(\overline{\{\underline x\}}= \operatorname{Spec}(A/\underline x)\) as a scheme. It follows that

\[ (\mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}}{}_{|U'})(U')=\operatorname{Frac} (A/\underline x), \]

the ring of fractions of \(A/\underline x\), and

\[ i'_\ast(\mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}}{}_{|U'})(X')=\operatorname{Frac}(A/\underline x). \]

But \(\operatorname{Frac}(A/\underline x)\) is not an \(A\)-module of finite type since \(\underline x\) is different from the maximal ideal of \(A\). Whence a contradiction.

Suppose \(n>1\) and the result established for \(n'<n\). By the induction hypothesis, for every \(x\in U\) such that \(c(x)=1\), we have \(x\not\in\operatorname{Ass} F_x\). Let such an \(x\), and let \(y\in\overline{\{x\}}\cap Y\) such that \(x\) follows \(y\), i.e. \(\dim\mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y}=1\). We perform the base change \(v\colon\operatorname{Spec} (\mathscr{O}_{X, y})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) keeping the notations of diagram (2.7). We find, applying (EGA III 1.4.15), isomorphisms:

\[ v^\ast(\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(F{}_{|U}) \simeq \operatorname{R}^pi'_\ast({v'}^\ast(F{}_{|U}))\text{, }p\in\mathbf{Z}. \]

We thus reduce to the case where \(X\) is the spectrum of a local ring \(A\) in which \(x\) is a prime ideal of dimension \(1\), i.e. \(\dim A/x=1\). Then set \(F'=\underline{\Gamma}_Y(F)\) and \(F''=F/F'\).

We see that \(F_x\simeq F''_x\) and that \(y\not\in \operatorname{Ass} F''\). Moreover \(F'{}_{|U}=0\) whence, by the exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast\), isomorphisms:

\[ \operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(F{}_{|U})\simeq \operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(F''{}_{|U})\text{, }p\in\mathbf{Z}. \]

Since \(n>1\), we deduce that neither \(x\) nor \(y\) belongs to \(\operatorname{Ass} F''\). Now these are the only prime ideals of \(A\) which contain \(x\); it follows (III 2.1) that there exists an element \(g\in x\) which is \(M\)-regular, where we have set \(F=\widetilde{M}\), \(M=F(X)\). Whence an exact sequence:

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\xrightarrow{g'} M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]

in which \(g'\) denotes the homothety with ratio \(g\) in \(M\). By the exact homology sequence, we see that:

\(\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(\widetilde{N}{}_{|U})\) is coherent for \(p<n-1\),

hence, by the induction hypothesis, \(\operatorname{prof}(\widetilde{N})_x\geqslant n-1\), hence \(\operatorname{prof} F_x\geqslant n\), QED.

blocks.html: 2 The finiteness theorem

blocks.title: Theorem 2.1
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.1

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, \(Y\) a closed part of \(X\) and \(F\) a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. Suppose that \(X\) is locally immersible in a regular prescheme1. Let \(i\in\mathbf{Z}\). Suppose that:

  1. for every \(x\in U=X-Y\), we have

    \[ \operatorname{H}^{i-c(x)}({F}_x)=0, \]

    where we have set:

    \[ \tag{2.1} c(x)=\operatorname{codim} (\overline{\{x\}}\cap Y, \overline{\{x\}}). \]

    Then:

  2. \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i_Y(F)\) is coherent.

blocks.title: Corollary 2.2
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.2

Under the hypotheses of the preceding theorem, condition a) is equivalent to:

  1. c)

    for every \(x\in U\) such that \(c(x)=1\), we have \(\operatorname{H}^{i-1}(F_x)=0\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.3

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme, locally immersible in a regular prescheme, \(Y\) a closed part of \(X\), \(F\) a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module, \(n\) an integer. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. for every \(x\in U\), we have \(\operatorname{prof} F_x>n-c(x)\);

  2. for every \(x\in U\) such that \(c(x)=1\), we have \(\operatorname{prof} F_x\geqslant n\);

  3. for every \(i\in \mathbf{Z}\), \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^i_Y(F)\) is coherent if \(i\leqslant n\).

  4. \(\operatorname{R}^ii_\ast(F_{|U})\) is coherent for \(i<n\) .
Suppose these results established when \(X\) is the spectrum of a regular noetherian ring \(A\) and when \(F\) is the sheaf associated to an \(A\)-module of finite projective dimension.

Let us first remark that, if \((X_j)_{j\in J}\) is an open covering of \(X\) by open sets, each of the conditions above is equivalent to the conjunction of the analogous conditions obtained by replacing \(X\) by \(X_j\), \(Y\) by \(Y_j=Y\cap X_j\) and \(F\) by \(F{|X_j}\). Indeed, only the conditions involving \(c(x)\) can present a difficulty. Let \(x\in U\). If \(x\in X_j\), set
\[ c_j(x)=\operatorname{codim}(X_j\cap \overline{\{x\}}\cap Y, X_j\cap\overline{\{x\}}), \]

we necessarily have \(c_j(x)\geqslant c(x)\). Let \(y\in \overline{\{x\}}\cap Y\) "give the codimension", i.e. such that \(c(x)=\dim \mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y}\), let \(X_j\) be an open set of the covering such that \(y\in X_j\), then \(x\in X_j\), hence \(c_j(x)=c(x)\), which allows us to conclude.

We choose a covering of \(X\) by open sets immersible in a regular prescheme. Applying what precedes, we see that we may suppose \(X\) closed in a regular \(X'\). The reduction to \(X'\) is then immediate.

We may therefore suppose \(X\) regular, and even affine by covering \(X\) by affine open sets. That we may suppose \(F=\widetilde{M}\), where \(M\) is of finite projective dimension, will result from the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.4

Let \(X\) be a regular noetherian prescheme. Let \(F\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. The function which to every \(x\in X\) associates the projective dimension of \(F_x\) is bounded above.

Indeed, let \(x\in X\) and let \(U\) be an affine open neighbourhood of \(x\). Let \(L^\bullet\) be a projective resolution of the module \(F(U)\), where the \(L^i\) are of finite type. By hypothesis, the ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) is regular, hence the projective dimension of \(F_x\) is finite; let \(d\) be this integer. Let
\[ K=\ker(L^{d-1}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} L^{d-2}). \]

The module \(K_x\) is free, since \(d\) is the projective dimension of \(F_x\) ([M], Ch. VI. Prop. 2.1). By (\(\mathrm{EGA}\, 0_{\textup{I}}\) 5.4.1 Errata), we deduce that the \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Module \(\widetilde{K}\) is free on a neighbourhood \(U'\) of \(x\), \(U'\subset U\). Choosing \(f\in \mathscr{O}_X(U)\) such that \(D(f)\subset U'\), we thus have a projective resolution of \(M_{f'}\) (\(M=F(U)\)):

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} K_f \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} (L^{d-1})_f\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \cdots \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M_f\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]
which proves that the function under study is upper semi-continuous. Now \(X\) is quasi-compact, whence the conclusion.

We now suppose \(X\) affine noetherian regular and we suppose that \(F=\widetilde{M}\), where \(M\) is an \(A\)-module of finite type, necessarily of finite projective dimension. We shall proceed in several steps. First, we find a condition d), equivalent to a), and prove that it is also equivalent to c). Then, with the help of the spectral sequence of the preceding n°, we prove d) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) b). It then remains to prove that (iii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii); indeed, (i) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) (ii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (iii) follows immediately from a) \(\mathchoice{\Longleftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}{\Leftrightarrow}\) c) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) b).
Let \(x\in U\); by hypothesis \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\) is a regular local ring; denoting by \(D\) the dualizing functor relative to the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{X, x}\), it follows from (V 2.1) that:
\[ D\operatorname{H}^{i-c(x)}(F_x)\approx \operatorname{Ext}^{d(x)-i}_{\mathscr{O}_{X, x}}(F_x, \mathscr{O}_{X, x}), \]
where we have set
\[ \tag{2.2} d(x)=\dim \mathscr{O}_{X, x} + c(x) = \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, x}+\operatorname{codim}(\overline{\{x\}}\cap Y, \overline{\{x\}}). \]
Now \(X\) is noetherian and \(F\) coherent, hence:
\[ \tag{2.3} D\operatorname{H}^{i-c(x)}(F_x)\approx (\operatorname{Ext}^{d(x)-i}_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, \mathscr{O}_X))_x. \]
Moreover, for a module to be zero, it is necessary and sufficient that its dual be so. For every \(q\in\mathbf{Z}\), set:

\[ \tag{2.4} \begin{cases} S_q=\operatorname{Supp} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^q_{\mathscr{O}_X}(F, \mathscr{O}_X), \\ S'_q=S_q\cap U\text{, (}U=X-Y\text{)}, \\ Z_q=\overline{S'_q}\cap Y. \end{cases} \]
From formula (2.3), it follows that a) and c) are respectively equivalent to

1. a\('\))

    for every \(q\in\mathbf{Z}\) and every \(x\in S'_q\), we have \(q+i\neq d(x)\).

  2. c\('\))

    for every \(q\in\mathbf{Z}\) and every \(x\in S'_q\), if \(c(x)=1\), we have \(q+i\neq d(x)\).
Here is the condition d) promised above:

1. d)

    for every \(q\in \mathbf{Z}\) and every \(y\in Z_q\), we have \(q+i\neq \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, y}\).
These conditions are equivalent:
a\('\)) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) c\('\)) for the record.

d) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) a\('\)). Indeed, let \(q\in \mathbf{Z}\) and let \(x\in S'_q\); let \(y\in\overline{\{x\}}\cap Y\) which "gives the codimension", i.e. such that:
\[ \tag{2.5} \dim \mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y}=\operatorname{codim}(\overline{\{x\}}\cap Y, \overline{\{x\}})=c(x). \]
From the fact that \(X\) is regular at \(y\), we deduce:
\[ \tag{2.6} \dim \mathscr{O}_{X, y}=d(x)\quad \text{(cf. (2.2)).} \]
But \(y\in\overline{\{x\}}\), hence \(y\in Z_q\), whence the conclusion.
c\('\)) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) d). Let \(q\in\mathbf{Z}\) and let \(y\in Z_q\). Let us provisionally admit that there exists \(x\in S'_q\) such that:
\[ \dim \mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y}=1\, \text{;} \]

(we also say that \(x\) follows \(y\)). It follows that \(c(x)=1\), since \(y\) "gives the codimension of \(\overline{\{x\}}\cap Y\) in \(\overline{\{x\}}\)", since \(x\not\in Y\). By c\('\)) we derive
\[ q+i\neq d(x). \]
Whence the conclusion, if we remark that \(d(x)=\dim \mathscr{O}_{X, y}\) (2.6). The admitted result is expressed in the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.5

Let \(X\) be a locally noetherian prescheme and let \(Y\) be a closed part of \(X\). Set \(U=X-Y\) and suppose that \(U\) is dense in \(X\). For every \(y\in Y\), there exists \(x\in U\) "which follows it", i.e. such that:

\[ y\in\overline{\{x\}}\text{ and } \dim\mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y}=1. \]
We have applied the lemma by taking for \(X\) the prescheme \(\overline{S'_q}\) and for \(Y\) the part \(Y\cap \overline{S'_q}\).

blocks.title: Proof of 2.5
blocks.html:
Proof of 2.5▾

There exists \(x\in U\) such that \(y\in\overline{\{x\}}\); let us therefore choose an \(x\in U\) such that \(y\in\overline{\{x\}}\) and such that \(\dim \mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y}=r\) is minimal. It must be proved that \(r=1\). Since \(x\) has been chosen so that every \(z\in\overline{\{x\}}\), \(z\neq x\), is in \(Y\), \(\{x\}\) is open in \(\operatorname{Spec}(\mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y})\). Whence the conclusion.

Q.E.D.
The second step consists in deducing b) from d).
Set \(D(Z_q)=\{\dim \mathscr{O}_{X, y} \mid y\in Z_q\}\). By d), we know that, for every \(q\in\mathbf{Z}\), we have \(q+i\not\in D(Z_q)\). We then apply 2.2, and we see that
\[ \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{q+i}_Y(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^q(F, \mathscr{O}_X), \mathscr{O}_X) \text{ is coherent.} \]
The initial term of the spectral sequence of the preceding n° is given by:
\[ \operatorname{E}^{p, q}_2=\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^p_Y(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{Ext}}}\nolimits^{-q}(F, \mathscr{O}_X), \mathscr{O}_X). \]

We deduce that \(\operatorname{E}^{p, q}_2\) is coherent for every \(p\in\mathbf{Z}\) and every \(q\in\mathbf{Z}\) such that \(p+q=i\). Now there are only a finite number of pairs \((p, q)\) such that \(p+q=i\), and this spectral sequence converges to \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^\ast_Y(F)\), whence the conclusion.
It remains for us to prove that (iii) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). Let us denote:
\[ i\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X \]
the canonical immersion of \(U\) into \(X\). Taking into account the exact homology sequence of the closed set \(Y\) (I 2.11) we see that (iii) is equivalent to:
(iv) \(\operatorname{R}^ii_\ast(F_{|U})\) is coherent for \(i<n\).
Indeed, we have an exact sequence:

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^0_Y(F)\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} F \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} i_\ast(F_{|U})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} \mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^1_Y(F) \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0. \]

Now \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^0_Y(F)\) is a quasi-coherent subsheaf of \(F\) which is coherent, hence is coherent. Therefore \(\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^1_Y(F)\) is coherent if and only if \(i_\ast(F_{|U})\) is. Moreover, if \(p>0\), the exact cohomology sequence of the closed set \(Y\) reduces to isomorphisms:
\[ \operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(F_{|U})\overset{\approx}{\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}}\mathop{\underline{\mathrm{H}}}\nolimits^{p+1}_Y(F). \]
We shall prove that (iv) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (ii). For this, let us recall (ii):
(ii) for every \(x\in U\) such that \(c(x)=1\), we have \(\operatorname{prof} F_x\geqslant n\).
Let us reason by induction on \(n\).
If \(n=0\), the two conditions are empty.

If \(n=1\), we suppose that \(i_\ast(F{|U})\) is coherent. Let us reason by contradiction and suppose that there exists \(x\in U\) such that \(c(x)=1\) and \(\operatorname{prof} F_x=0\), i.e. \(x\in \operatorname{Ass} F_x\). Let \(y\in\overline{\{x\}}\cap Y\) such that \(\dim \mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y}=1\). Set:
\[ A=\mathscr{O}_{X, y}\text{ and } X'=\operatorname{Spec}(A). \]
Let us perform the base change \(v\colon X'\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\), which is flat.
\[ \tag{2.7} \begin{array}{c} \xymatrix@C=5em{ U'=X'\times_X U\ar[r]^-{v'}\ar[d]_{i'} & U\ar[d]^i \\ X'\ar[r]^-v & \,X\,. } \end{array} \]

The morphism \(i\) is separated (since it is an immersion), and of finite type (since it is an open immersion and \(X\) is locally noetherian), the base change is flat hence (EGA III 1.4.15) we have an isomorphism:
\[ \tag{2.8} v^\ast(i_\ast(F|U) \approx i'_\ast({v'}^\ast(F_{|U})). \]
Let us denote by \(\underline x\) (resp. \(\underline y\)) the ideal of \(A\) corresponding to \(x\) (resp. \(y\)).

Set \({\operatorname{G}}={v'}^\ast(F{}_{|U})\); \({\operatorname{G}}\) is coherent and \(\underline{x}\in\operatorname{Ass} {\operatorname{G}}\), hence there exists a monomorphism \(\mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}}\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} {\operatorname{G}}\), and consequently \(i'_\ast(\mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}}{}_{|U'})\) is coherent. By the choice of \(y\), \(\dim A/\underline x=1\), and consequently the support of \(\mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}}\) is reduced to \(\overline{\{\underline x\}}= \{\underline x\}\cup \{\underline y\}\), since \(\overline{\{\underline x\}}= \operatorname{Spec}(A/\underline x)\) as a scheme. It follows that
\[ (\mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}}{}_{|U'})(U')=\operatorname{Frac} (A/\underline x), \]
the ring of fractions of \(A/\underline x\), and
\[ i'_\ast(\mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}}{}_{|U'})(X')=\operatorname{Frac}(A/\underline x). \]
But \(\operatorname{Frac}(A/\underline x)\) is not an \(A\)-module of finite type since \(\underline x\) is different from the maximal ideal of \(A\). Whence a contradiction.

Suppose \(n>1\) and the result established for \(n'<n\). By the induction hypothesis, for every \(x\in U\) such that \(c(x)=1\), we have \(x\not\in\operatorname{Ass} F_x\). Let such an \(x\), and let \(y\in\overline{\{x\}}\cap Y\) such that \(x\) follows \(y\), i.e. \(\dim\mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y}=1\). We perform the base change \(v\colon\operatorname{Spec} (\mathscr{O}_{X, y})\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) keeping the notations of diagram (2.7). We find, applying (EGA III 1.4.15), isomorphisms:
\[ v^\ast(\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(F{}_{|U}) \simeq \operatorname{R}^pi'_\ast({v'}^\ast(F{}_{|U}))\text{, }p\in\mathbf{Z}. \]

We thus reduce to the case where \(X\) is the spectrum of a local ring \(A\) in which \(x\) is a prime ideal of dimension \(1\), i.e. \(\dim A/x=1\). Then set \(F'=\underline{\Gamma}_Y(F)\) and \(F''=F/F'\).
We see that \(F_x\simeq F''_x\) and that \(y\not\in \operatorname{Ass} F''\). Moreover \(F'{}_{|U}=0\) whence, by the exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast\), isomorphisms:
\[ \operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(F{}_{|U})\simeq \operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(F''{}_{|U})\text{, }p\in\mathbf{Z}. \]

Since \(n>1\), we deduce that neither \(x\) nor \(y\) belongs to \(\operatorname{Ass} F''\). Now these are the only prime ideals of \(A\) which contain \(x\); it follows (III 2.1) that there exists an element \(g\in x\) which is \(M\)-regular, where we have set \(F=\widetilde{M}\), \(M=F(X)\). Whence an exact sequence:

\[ 0\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} M\xrightarrow{g'} M\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} N \mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} 0, \]
in which \(g'\) denotes the homothety with ratio \(g\) in \(M\). By the exact homology sequence, we see that:
\(\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(\widetilde{N}{}_{|U})\) is coherent for \(p<n-1\),
hence, by the induction hypothesis, \(\operatorname{prof}(\widetilde{N})_x\geqslant n-1\), hence \(\operatorname{prof} F_x\geqslant n\), QED.

footnotes.html: This condition can be generalized to the hypothesis of the existence, locally on \(X\), of a "dualizing complex", in the sense defined in R. Hartshorne, Residues and duality (cited in Exp. IV p. 23).

pages.title: Applications
pages.html:
3 Applications

From these results one deduces a coherence condition for the higher direct images of a coherent sheaf under a morphism which is not proper.

Theorem 3.1

Let \(f\colon X\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} Y\) be a morphism of preschemes. Suppose that \(Y\) is locally noetherian and that \(f\) is proper. Suppose that \(X\) is locally immersible in a regular prescheme. Let \(n\in\mathbf{Z}\). Let \(U\) be an open set of \(X\) and let \(F\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Module. Suppose that, for every \(x\in U\) such that \(\operatorname{codim}(\overline{\{x\}}\cap (X-U), \overline{\{x\}})\!=\!1\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} F_x\geqslant n\). Then the \(\mathscr{O}_Y\)-Modules \(\operatorname{R}^p(f\circ g)_\ast(F)\) are coherent for \(p<n\), where \(g\) is the canonical immersion of \(U\) into \(X\).

Indeed, there exists a LERAY spectral sequence whose abutment is \(\operatorname{R}^\ast(f\circ g)_\ast(F)\) and whose initial term is given by:

\[ \operatorname{E}^{p, q}_2=R^pf_\ast(\operatorname{R}^qg_\ast(F)). \]

Moreover, there exists a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module \({\operatorname{G}}\) such that \({\operatorname{G}}{}_{|U}\simeq F\), (EGA I 9.4.3). It then follows from the preceding section that condition (iv) is satisfied, i.e. that \(\operatorname{R}^qg_\ast({\operatorname{G}}{}_{|U})\) is coherent for \(q<n\). One then applies the finiteness theorem of EGA III 3.2.1 to \(f\) and to the sheaves \(\operatorname{R}^qg_\ast(F)\), and one finds that \(\operatorname{E}^{p, q}_2\) is coherent for \(q<n\), whence the conclusion.

Proposition 3.2

Let \(X\) be a prescheme that is locally noetherian and locally immersible in a regular prescheme. Let \(U\) be an open set of \(X\) and let \(i\colon U\mathchoice{\longrightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow}{\rightarrow} X\) be the canonical immersion. Let \(n\in\mathbf{Z}\). Finally, let \(F\) be a coherent and COHEN-MACAULAY \(\mathscr{O}_U\)-Module (on \(U\)!). The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. (a)

    \(\operatorname{R}^pi_\ast(F)\) is coherent for \(p<n\);

  2. (b)

    for every irreducible component \(S'\) of the closure \(\overline S\) of the support \(S\) of \(F\), one has:

    \[ \operatorname{codim} (S'\cap (X-U), S')>n. \]

Let \({\operatorname{G}}\) be a coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module such that \({\operatorname{G}}{}_{|U}\simeq F\) (EGA I 9.4.3). Applying corollary 2.3 to \({\operatorname{G}}\), one finds that condition (a) is equivalent to (c):

  1. (c)

    for every \(x\in S\), one has \(\operatorname{prof} F_x>n-c(x)\), with

    \[ c(x)=\operatorname{codim}(\overline{\{x\}}\cap Y, \overline{\{x\}}). \]

(a) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (b). Indeed, let \(S'\) be an irreducible component of \(\overline S\) and let \(s\) be its generic point. Since \(F\) is COHEN-MACAULAY, one has \(\operatorname{prof} F_s=\dim \mathscr{O}_{S, s}=0\). Moreover, \(\overline{\{s\}}=S'\), whence the conclusion.

(b) \(\mathchoice{\Longrightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}{\Rightarrow}\) (a). Let \(x\in S\) and let \(S'\) be an irreducible component of \(\overline{S}\) such that \(x\in S'\). Let \(s\) be the generic point of \(S'\). Since \(F\) is COHEN-MACAULAY, we know that:

\[ \operatorname{prof} F_x=\dim \mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{s\}}, x}. \]

If \(c(x)=+\infty\), there is nothing to prove. Otherwise, there exists \(y\in Y\cap \overline{\{x\}}\), such that:

\[ c(x)=\dim \mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y}. \]

Now \(\mathscr{O}_{X, y}\) is a quotient of a regular local ring by hypothesis, hence:

\[ \dim \mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{s\}}, y}=\dim \mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{s\}}, x} +\dim \mathscr{O}_{\overline{\{x\}}, y}>n. \]

Q.E.D.

[M]
H. Cartan – S. Eilenberg, Homological Algebra, Princeton University Press, 1956.
